planting.note: Plant hardwood cuttings in early spring or container plants in spring in well-drained, average to fertile soil; summer grape is very adaptable to soil type and pH and requires only pruning to maintain fruit production. Train on a strong trellis, arbor, or fence.

directions: Plant in well-drained to average soil of any pH in full sun; summer grape is one of the most widespread wild grape species in eastern North America, climbing high into the forest canopy from New England to Florida and west to Nebraska. The large, deeply lobed leaves resemble European wine grape leaves and provide dense, ornamental screening on arbors and fences. Clusters of small, blue-black, seedy but sweet-tart grapes ripen in late summer to fall and are consumed by over 90 species of birds and mammals. The grapes are edible for humans and have been used to make juice, jelly, and wine — they are tart when underripe but improve after frost. Summer grape is a host plant for multiple moth and butterfly species including the grapeleaf skeletonizer. Prune back in late winter to a few buds on the main stems to encourage fruiting shoots.

planting.note: Plant hardwood cuttings in spring in moist to average, well-drained soil; riverbank grape is one of the most cold-hardy native grapes and has been used extensively in cold-climate wine grape breeding programs. It prefers moist, fertile bottomland soils but adapts to upland conditions.

directions: Plant in moist to well-drained, fertile, loamy soil in full sun along riverbanks, fences, and arbors; riverbank grape is native to streambanks and forest edges across most of North America, from Quebec to Texas, and is one of the most cold-hardy wild grapes — surviving to zone 2. The small, highly fragrant flowers in spring are intensely honey-scented and attract numerous pollinators. The small, blue-black, tart grapes ripen after the first fall frost (hence 'frost grape'), becoming slightly sweeter and less astringent with cold exposure. This species has been critically important in wine breeding — it is a parent of numerous cold-hardy wine grape cultivars including 'Marquette', 'Frontenac', and 'Frontenac Gris' developed for cold northern climates. Also an important source of phylloxera-resistant rootstock for European wine grapes.

planting.note: Plant container stock or rooted cuttings in spring in moist to well-drained soil in any light condition; peppervine is one of the most shade-tolerant native vines and grows naturally in the forest understory and along shaded streambanks.

directions: Plant in moist to average, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun along fences, trellises, and at woodland edges; peppervine is a native southeastern vine distinguished from grapes and other ampelopsis by its twice-compound leaves — the leaflets are themselves divided, giving the foliage a lacy, highly decorative texture quite unlike any other native vine. The small berries pass through shades of green, lavender, white, and finally dark purple-black as they ripen in fall — a stunning polychrome effect on the same cluster that makes this plant highly ornamental. The berries are important food for mockingbirds, bluebirds, and other fruit-eating birds in fall and winter. The plant tolerates partial shade better than most fruiting vines and is an excellent choice for naturalized areas in the woodland garden or at the edge of a forest.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring after last frost in well-drained to dry soil in full sun; sorrelvine dies back to the ground after hard freezes in zone 8-9 but re-emerges vigorously from thickened roots in spring. It grows naturally on rocky limestone slopes and caliche in central and south Texas.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, rocky, alkaline or neutral soil in full sun in zones 7–11; sorrelvine is a native Texas vine of the same family as grapes, naturally scrambling over limestone slopes, rocky draws, and caliche outcrops across central and south Texas. The trifoliate leaves superficially resemble poison ivy — enough to cause alarm — but sorrelvine has tendrils (poison ivy doesn't) and causes no skin irritation. The small black berries are eaten by birds but toxic to humans. Extremely heat and drought tolerant once established, with thickened roots that store water. In zone 9a it makes a vigorous summer vine that provides quick cover on a fence or trellis during the hottest part of the year. Dies back after hard frost but re-sprouts reliably from the base in spring.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination; or cold-stratify seeds for 60 days and sow indoors in late winter. This vine dies back to the ground in winter and re-emerges from the root in spring. Plant in well-drained, sandy or rocky soil.

directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy, lean, acidic to neutral soil in full sun; netleaf leather flower is a slender native vine of the southeastern Coastal Plain that scrambles through shrubs and over fences with modest vigor. The unique, urn-shaped flowers have thick, leathery tepals in shades of dull purple with pale-veined markings — resembling tiny pitchers that hang from the vine stems in summer. Unlike the more showy garden clematis, this species is subtle and discovered on close inspection. The feathery seed heads that follow are silvery and attractive. The netted or reticulated (net-like) veins of the leaves give the species its name. This is one of the native leather flower clematis of the Gulf Coastal Plain (related to C. crispa and C. viorna) and a rare but valuable plant for a low-water, naturalistic southern garden.
